## Context and Problem Statement

We no longer want to depend on SQLCipher and want to use SQLite directly for build complexity and concerns over the long term future of the rust bindings. The encryption approach taken by SQLCipher means that in practice, the entire database is decrypted at startup, even if the logins functionality is not interacted with, defeating some of the benefits of using an encrypted database.

The per-field encryption in autofill, which we are planning to replicate in logins, separates the storage and encryption logic by limiting the storage layer to the management of encrypted data. Applying this approach in logins will break the existing validation and deduping code so we need a way to implement per-field encryption while supporting the validation and de-duping behavior.

## Decision Drivers

* Addressing previously identified deficiencies in the logins API while we are breaking the API for the encryption work
* Continuing to support the existing logins validation and deduping logic
* Avoiding the implementation of new security approaches that may require additional time and security resources
* Establishing a standard encyrption approach across components


## Considered Options

* Option 1 - Reduce the API functions that require the encryption key and pass the key to the remaining functions
* Option 2 - Keep the general shape of the API that is in place now - the app can pass the encryption key at any time to "unlock" the API, and re-lock it at any time, but the API in its entirety is only available when unlocked
human_decision:
# Update Logins API

* Status: accepted
* Date: 2021-06-17

Technical Story: [#4101](https://github.com/mozilla/application-services/issues/4101)

## Decision Outcome

Chosen Option: "Reduce the API functions that require the encryption key and pass the key to the remaining functions" because it will not require a security review as similar to the approach we have established in the codebase.


## Pros and Cons of the Options

### Option 1 - Reduce the API functions that require the encryption key and pass the key to the remaining functions

* Description

  Currently the below logins API functions would require the per-field encryption key:
    - [add](https://github.com/mozilla/application-services/blob/1248a352cb2701b92395f2783bee8a88d18de455/components/logins/src/db.rs#L362)
    - [update](https://github.com/mozilla/application-services/blob/1248a352cb2701b92395f2783bee8a88d18de455/components/logins/src/db.rs#L611)
    - [get_all](https://github.com/mozilla/application-services/blob/1248a352cb2701b92395f2783bee8a88d18de455/components/logins/src/db.rs#L273)
    - [get_by_base_domain](https://github.com/mozilla/application-services/blob/1248a352cb2701b92395f2783bee8a88d18de455/components/logins/src/db.rs#L279)
    - [get_by_id](https://github.com/mozilla/application-services/blob/1248a352cb2701b92395f2783bee8a88d18de455/components/logins/src/db.rs#L330)
    - [check_valid_with_no_dupes](https://github.com/mozilla/application-services/blob/1248a352cb2701b92395f2783bee8a88d18de455/components/logins/src/db.rs#L663)
    - [potential_dupes_ignoring_username](https://github.com/mozilla/application-services/blob/1248a352cb2701b92395f2783bee8a88d18de455/components/logins/src/db.rs#L721)
    - [import_multiple](https://github.com/mozilla/application-services/blob/1248a352cb2701b92395f2783bee8a88d18de455/components/logins/src/db.rs#L455)

      **Note:**
        - Functions related to sync have been omitted as it is assumed they will have access to decrypted data.
        - The `get_all`, `get_by_base_domain`, and `get_by_id` functions will require the encryption key because they call the validate and fixup logic, not because we want to return logins with decrypted data.

  Propsed changes:
    - Combine the `add` and `update` functions into a new `add_or_update` function
      - This will allow the removal of consumer code that distinguishes when a login record should be created or updated
      - **Note:** This function needs the encryption key for the fixup and deduping logic _and_ for continued support of the accurate population of the `time_password_changed` field
    - Pass the per-field encryption key to the `import_multiple` function
      - This function will be removed once the Fennec to Fenix migration period ends
    - Remove both the `potential_dupes_ignoring_username` and `check_valid_with_no_dupes` from the API
      - Neither function is called in Firefox iOS
      - Android Components uses both to provide validation and de-duping before logins are added or updated so we can eliminate the need to externalize these functions by replicating this logic in the new `add_or_update` function
    - Create a `decrypt_and_fixup_login` function that both decrypts a login _and_ performs the validate and fixup logic
      - This will eliminate the need for the `get_all`, `get_by_base_domain`, and `get_by_id` API functions to perform the fixup logic

    Making the above changes will reduce the API functions requiring the encryption key to the following:
    - `add_or_update`
    - `decrypt_and_fixup_login`
    - `import_multiple`

* Pros
  * Improves the logins API for consumers by combining add/update functionality (see [#3899](https://github.com/mozilla/application-services/issues/3899) for details)
  * Removes redundant validation and de-duping logic in consumer code
  * Uses the same encryption model as autofill so there is consistency in our approaches
* Cons
  * Requires consumer code to both encrypt login fields _and_ pass the encryption key when calling either `add_or_update` and `import_multiple`


### Option 2 - Implement a different key management approach

* Description

  Unlike the first option, the publicly exposed login API would only handle decrypted login records and all encryption is internal (which works because we always have the key). Any attempt to use the API will fail as the login records are not encrypted or decrypted if the key is not available.

  Proposed changes:
  - Combine the `add` and `update` functions into `add_or_update`
  - Remove both the `potential_dupes_ignoring_username` and `check_valid_with_no_dupes` from the API

* Pros
  * Prevents the consumer from having to encrypt or decrypt login records
  * Maintains our current fixup and validation approach
  * Improves the logins API for consumers by combining add/update functionality
  * Removes redundant validation and de-duping logic in consumer code
* Cons
  * Makes us responsible for securing the encryption key and will most likely require a security review

* [Logins Validate and Fixup Call Tree](https://docs.google.com/drawings/d/1GZExe9lNpNDCoywpmg4RxHHNoqyaQ2CapbUyoM3K-KQ/edit?usp=sharing)

## Context and Problem Statement

Facing the need to create wrappers to make using the Nunjucks components ported from hmrc-frontend more
straightforward and idiomatic on the Scala/Play MDTP platform, how should we name these components?

The components ported from hmrc-frontend include
* implementations of govuk-frontend components that support the Welsh language e.g. hmrcHeader, hmrcFooter
* components that meet specific HMRC needs e.g. hmrcBanner, hmrcInternalHeader
* components that have not been standardised by GDS but are needed by HMRC e.g. hmrcNotificationBadge, hmrcTimeoutDialog

Being entirely presentational and ported from Nunjucks means they cannot leverage features built into Scala/Play nor
make use of any MDTP platform knowledge that would simplify their use on MDTP. For example,
* they do not and should not know the url structure for common MDTP components e.g. tracking-consent-frontend, 
 contact-frontend or the accessibility-statement-frontend, services that need to wired in on every public-facing service via 
  standard headers and footers.
* they cannot make use of Play's i18n features and automatic language wiring
* they cannot make use of any knowledge encoded in the requests users make e.g. the request URL, referrer URL, cookies
  or headers.

For the above reasons, we are creating wrappers that implement standardised Play/platform wiring, to avoid teams having to
duplicate this wiring across 100s of repositories. Once implemented we will encourage teams to use these helpers rather than
the underlying Nunjucks ports.

## Decision Drivers

* The fact that the ideal component names have already been taken by the Nunjucks components.
* The preference for not relying on the package name to differentiate the components
* The preference for not repeating the word Helper in the package and component name
* The preference for having names that are unsurprising and will encourage use of the helper in preference to the underlying Nunjucks
component.
* The preference for consistency in naming across the components

## Considered Options

* Suffix with 'Helper' e.g. hmrcReportTechnicalIssue/hmrcReportTechnicalIssueHelper
* Suffix with 'Wrapper' e.g. hmrcReportTechnicalIssue/hmrcReportTechnicalIssueWrapper
* Use the same name as the Nunjucks component and differentiate using the package name
 e.g. uk.gov.hmrc.hmrcfrontend.views.html.components.hmrcReportTechnicalIssue/uk.gov.hmrc.hmrcfrontend.views.html.helpers.hmrcReportTechnicalIssue
* Use a variation of the Nunjucks component name e.g. hmrcReportTechnicalIssue/hmrcGetHelpWithThisPage
human_decision:
# Use the suffix Helper for helper components

* Status: accepted
* Date: 2020-12-03

Technical Story: PLATUI-792

## Decision Outcome

Chosen option: "Suffix with 'Helper'", because this is the only option the team are happy with bearing in mind the decision
drivers listed above.

### Positive Consequences

* We have a consistent naming scheme
* Friction around needing to come up with new names for helpers reduced, increasing speed of development.

### Negative Consequences

* There is a risk that service teams will not know the helpers exist
* Repeating the word Helper in the component and having it in the package name is ugly

reason: The affirmative side's argument to suffix with 'Wrapper' effectively addresses the key requirements and constraints, such as distinct naming, consistency, and readability, without relying on package names. It provides a clear and intuitive naming convention that aligns with the project's goals and facilitates integration with Scala/Play features. The negative side's approach, while offering modularity and scalability, may lead to confusion and lacks immediate clarity in component names, which are crucial for developers' understanding and ease of use.
message_history:
The debate centers around determining the best naming convention for wrappers around Nunjucks components that have been ported for use on the Scala/Play MDTP platform. The key requirements and constraints include distinct naming, consistency, avoiding reliance on package names, avoiding repetition of "Helper," facilitating integration with frameworks, flexibility for future changes, and readability.

The considered options are:
1. Suffix with "Helper"
2. Suffix with "Wrapper"
3. Differentiate using package name
4. Variation of the Nunjucks component name

The recommendation is to suffix with "Wrapper" (Option 2). This approach is favored because it provides clear distinction, maintains consistency, avoids reliance on package names, enhances readability, and supports flexibility for future changes. It aligns with the preferences for simplicity, consistency, and clarity while ensuring integration with Scala/Play features without unnecessary term repetition.

**Arguments in Favor of Suffixing with "Wrapper":**
- **Distinct Naming**: Adding "Wrapper" clearly distinguishes the new components from their original Nunjucks counterparts, fulfilling the requirement for distinct naming.
- **Consistency**: Using a uniform suffix across all components ensures consistency, making it easier for developers to recognize and understand the purpose of each component.
- **Avoids Package Name Reliance**: By not relying on package names for differentiation, this approach ensures that the distinction is immediately visible in the component name itself.
- **Readability and Understandability**: The term "Wrapper" is intuitive and indicative of the component's role, enhancing readability and making the purpose of the component clear to developers.
- **Flexibility for Future Changes**: The naming convention is adaptable, allowing for easy updates and additions as the platform evolves.

**Counterarguments:**
- **Suffix with "Helper"**: While this option also provides distinction, it conflicts with the requirement to avoid repeating "Helper" in both package and component names.
- **Differentiate Using Package Name**: This approach could lead to confusion if developers are not familiar with the package structure, and it does not provide immediate clarity in the component name itself.
- **Variation of the Nunjucks Component Name**: Modifying the original name slightly might not provide enough distinction and could lead to inconsistencies in naming.

In conclusion, suffixing with "Wrapper" is the most effective solution as it meets all the key requirements and constraints, providing a clear, consistent, and intuitive naming convention for the wrapper components.
While the recommendation to suffix with "Wrapper" (Option 2) has its merits, I propose that differentiating using the package name (Option 3) could be a more effective solution. Here are the reasons for this perspective:

**Arguments in Favor of Differentiating Using Package Name:**

1. **Enhanced Modularity and Organization**: By using package names to differentiate, the codebase can be better organized. This approach allows for grouping related components together, making it easier to manage and navigate the code.

2. **Scalability**: As the project grows, using package names can help manage a larger number of components without cluttering the global namespace. This is particularly beneficial in large projects where naming conflicts might arise.

3. **Integration with Frameworks**: Scala/Play frameworks often utilize package structures for organization. Aligning with this practice can facilitate smoother integration and adherence to framework conventions.

4. **Flexibility for Future Changes**: Package-based differentiation allows for more flexibility in restructuring or expanding the codebase. New components can be added to existing packages or new packages can be created without altering existing component names.

5. **Avoids Redundancy**: This approach avoids the redundancy of adding a suffix to each component name, which can become cumbersome and repetitive, especially if the component's role is already clear from the context.

**Counterarguments to Suffixing with "Wrapper":**

- **Potential for Over-Simplification**: While "Wrapper" is clear, it might oversimplify the role of the component, especially if the wrapper adds significant functionality or transformation beyond just wrapping.

- **Namespace Clutter**: Adding "Wrapper" to every component name can lead to a cluttered namespace, especially if there are many components, making it harder to quickly identify specific components.

- **Lack of Contextual Clarity**: In some cases, the term "Wrapper" might not provide enough context about the specific functionality or purpose of the component, leading to potential misunderstandings.

In conclusion, while suffixing with "Wrapper" offers simplicity and immediate clarity, differentiating using package names provides a more scalable, organized, and framework-aligned approach. It enhances modularity and flexibility, which are crucial for maintaining and expanding a large codebase over time.

message_history:
In evaluating the best Continuous Integration (CI) solution for the winery, we have three main contenders: TravisCI, CircleCI, and Jenkins. Each has its strengths and weaknesses based on the winery's specific needs and requirements.

### Key Decision Drivers

1. **Easy Configuration**:
   - **TravisCI**: Known for its simplicity, especially with GitHub projects, using a straightforward `.travis.yml` file.
   - **CircleCI**: Offers easy configuration with its `.circleci/config.yml` file and provides high customizability.
   - **Jenkins**: Requires a more complex initial setup and manual configuration, which can be a barrier for teams without technical expertise.

2. **Free CI Solution**:
   - **TravisCI**: Offers a free plan with unlimited builds for public and private repositories, though concurrent builds are limited.
   - **CircleCI**: Provides a free plan with 600 minutes of build time per month for one user, which is suitable for small projects.
   - **Jenkins**: Completely free and open-source, but hosting and maintenance costs should be considered if not self-hosted.

3. **Customizable Logging**:
   - **TravisCI**: Allows some level of logging customization through its configuration file.
   - **CircleCI**: Offers detailed logs and supports customizable logging, with integration options for external services.
   - **Jenkins**: Highly customizable logging capabilities, including log configurations, levels, output, and integration with various tools.

4. **Support for Automated Builds, Tests, and Deployments**:
   - **TravisCI**: Supports automated builds and tests, but deployments require additional setup.
   - **CircleCI**: Strongly supports automating build, test, and deployment processes.
   - **Jenkins**: Capable of handling automated processes through its extensive plugin ecosystem.

5. **Integration with Existing Development Tools and Platforms**:
   - **TravisCI**: Tightly integrated with GitHub.
   - **CircleCI**: Integrates well with GitHub and supports other version control systems.
   - **Jenkins**: Can integrate with almost any development tool or platform via plugins.

6. **Security, Compliance, and Collaboration Features**:
   - **TravisCI**: Offers role-based access control, encryption, and compliance features.
   - **CircleCI**: Provides strong security features, including encryption and compliance support (e.g., HIPAA, SOC 2).
   - **Jenkins**: Customizable security through plugins, but requires careful setup for compliance.

### Evaluation Summary

- **TravisCI**: Excels in ease of use and GitHub integration but may lack in customization and advanced features.
- **CircleCI**: Balances ease of use with powerful options, making it suitable for complex projects.
- **Jenkins**: Offers extensive customizability and cost-effectiveness but requires technical expertise for setup and maintenance.

### Recommendation

Given the winery's needs, **CircleCI** is recommended due to its balanced approach to ease of use, customization, integration capabilities, and strong support for CI/CD processes, security, and compliance. It effectively handles complex workflows, integrates with various platforms, and enforces security and compliance standards, making it a compelling choice. However, the final decision should consider the team's expertise, project complexity, and future scalability needs.
While CircleCI is a strong contender for the winery's CI needs, I would argue that Jenkins might be a more suitable choice, especially considering certain aspects that could be crucial for the winery's long-term strategy and specific requirements.

### Reasons for Choosing Jenkins

1. **Cost-Effectiveness**:
   - Jenkins is completely free and open-source, which means there are no limitations on build minutes or concurrent builds. This can be particularly advantageous for a winery that might scale its operations and require extensive CI/CD processes without incurring additional costs.

2. **Customizability and Flexibility**:
   - Jenkins offers unparalleled customizability through its extensive plugin ecosystem. This allows the winery to tailor the CI/CD pipeline to its specific needs, integrating with virtually any tool or platform. This flexibility can be crucial for adapting to future technological changes or integrating with niche tools specific to the winery industry.

3. **Scalability**:
   - Jenkins is highly scalable, making it suitable for both small and large projects. As the winery grows, Jenkins can easily accommodate increased demands without the constraints of a free tier, as seen with CircleCI.

4. **Integration Capabilities**:
   - While CircleCI integrates well with GitHub and other version control systems, Jenkins can integrate with almost any development tool or platform via plugins. This broad integration capability ensures that the winery can seamlessly connect its existing tools and future technologies.

5. **Security and Compliance**:
   - Although Jenkins requires careful setup for security and compliance, its customizable security features through plugins can be tailored to meet specific compliance requirements. This can be particularly important for wineries that need to adhere to industry-specific regulations.

6. **Community and Support**:
   - Jenkins has a large and active community, providing a wealth of resources, plugins, and support. This community-driven approach can be beneficial for troubleshooting and finding solutions to unique challenges the winery might face.

### Conclusion

While CircleCI offers a balanced approach with ease of use and strong support for CI/CD processes, Jenkins provides a more robust and flexible solution that can grow with the winery's needs. Its cost-effectiveness, scalability, and extensive integration capabilities make it a compelling choice for a winery looking to invest in a long-term CI/CD strategy. The decision should ultimately consider the team's technical expertise and willingness to invest in setting up and maintaining Jenkins, but its potential benefits could outweigh these initial challenges.

message_history:
In evaluating the best proposed solution for representing samples in a system, we must consider the specific requirements and constraints of the application in question. The analysis provided outlines two primary approaches: using a single tuple or separate vectors for domain and range objects, each with its own advantages and disadvantages.

**Single Tuple Approach:**
- **Advantages:** Potentially better compression ratios.
- **Disadvantages:** May compromise conceptual integrity and performance, especially with complex or large datasets.

**Separate Vectors Approach:**
- **Advantages:** Facilitates data preprocessing, aligns with database structures, and offers flexibility with multidimensional data.
- **Disadvantages:** May not achieve the same compression ratios as single tuples.

**Common Considerations:**
- Scalability, interoperability, and development ease are crucial factors that influence the choice of representation.

**Conclusion and Recommendations:**
The analysis suggests that the best solution is not strictly one approach over the other but rather a flexible strategy that can adapt to various use cases. The recommendations include:

1. **Hybrid Approach:** Combining the benefits of both single tuple and separate vectors representations can provide a balanced solution that leverages the strengths of each method.
2. **Modular Design:** Designing the system with modularity allows for easy switching between different representation approaches, enhancing adaptability.
3. **Extensive Testing:** Conducting thorough testing ensures that the chosen representation meets performance, integrity, and correctness requirements across different scenarios.

Given these considerations, the best proposed solution is the adoption of a **Hybrid Approach** supported by a **Modular Design** and **Extensive Testing**. This approach allows developers to tailor the representation to the specific needs of their application, ensuring flexibility, scalability, and ease of integration with other systems.
While the hybrid approach, modular design, and extensive testing are indeed comprehensive strategies, I would argue that the best proposed solution should be more context-specific rather than adopting a generalized hybrid model. Here’s why:

1. **Application-Specific Needs:**
   - Different applications have unique requirements. For instance, in scientific computing where precision and performance are critical, the separate vectors approach might be more suitable due to its flexibility and alignment with database structures. Conversely, in data compression scenarios, the single tuple approach might be preferred for its better compression ratios.

2. **Complexity and Overhead:**
   - Implementing a hybrid approach and modular design can introduce additional complexity and overhead. This might not be justified for simpler applications where one approach clearly outperforms the other based on specific criteria like performance or ease of integration.

3. **Resource Constraints:**
   - Not all projects have the resources to conduct extensive testing or to develop a modular system that supports multiple representation strategies. In such cases, choosing the most appropriate single approach based on initial analysis could be more efficient.

4. **Scalability and Maintenance:**
   - While a hybrid approach offers flexibility, it can also complicate scalability and maintenance. A clear, singular approach might be easier to scale and maintain, especially in environments with limited technical support.

5. **Interoperability:**
   - Depending on the existing systems and tools, one approach might offer better interoperability. For example, if the system heavily relies on NoSQL databases, the separate vectors approach might naturally integrate better.

In conclusion, while the hybrid approach offers a balanced solution, the best proposed solution should be determined by the specific context and requirements of the application. A thorough initial analysis to choose either the single tuple or separate vectors approach, based on the application's primary needs, could be more effective and efficient. This targeted strategy minimizes unnecessary complexity and focuses resources on optimizing the chosen approach.

  actually used

* Always import manually on a case by case basis
  * Potentially error prone
  * Would require docs/pipeline for how to add new icons to system
* Do Nothing?

In evaluating the two options for managing icons in a React application—"Always Import Manually on a Case-by-Case Basis" and "Do Nothing"—it becomes clear that neither option is ideal on its own. The manual import method offers direct control and customization but is error-prone, time-consuming, and requires additional documentation and processes. On the other hand, doing nothing is simple and requires no immediate action but can lead to inconsistent icon management, potential legal issues, and performance impacts.

The content suggests a third, more structured approach: using libraries like `react-icons`. This method combines the benefits of both options while addressing their drawbacks. It offers scalability through SVG icons, compliance with open-source licensing, consistency in icon quality, and performance optimization. By streamlining the development process and minimizing potential legal and technical issues, this approach enhances overall application quality and user experience.

Therefore, the best proposed solution is to adopt a structured approach to icon management using libraries like `react-icons`. This method effectively balances control, efficiency, and compliance, making it the most comprehensive and practical choice for managing icons in a React application.
While the structured approach using libraries like `react-icons` is indeed a strong solution, I would argue that the decision should be context-dependent, and there might be scenarios where one of the original options could be more appropriate.

1. **Always Import Manually on a Case-by-Case Basis**: This approach, despite its drawbacks, might be suitable for smaller projects or teams that prioritize customization and have the resources to manage icons meticulously. In such cases, the ability to choose icons from any source and tailor them precisely to the application's needs could outweigh the potential for errors and the time investment required.

2. **Do Nothing**: For very early-stage projects or prototypes where speed and simplicity are paramount, doing nothing might be a viable short-term strategy. This approach allows developers to focus on core functionality without being bogged down by icon management. However, it's crucial to recognize that this is not sustainable in the long term due to the risks of inconsistency, legal issues, and performance problems.

Ultimately, while the structured approach using libraries like `react-icons` is generally the most balanced and efficient solution, the best choice depends on the specific needs, resources, and stage of the project. In some cases, the flexibility of manual imports or the simplicity of doing nothing might align better with the project's immediate goals. Therefore, it's essential to evaluate the context and requirements before deciding on the best approach.

## Context and Problem Statement

Azure Blob Storage will be used to store all files associated with permit application.  Both front stage and back stage users need to be able to read and upload documents to a 'case file'.

## Decision Drivers

* Back stage users should be able to see all files relating to a permit
* Front stage users should only be able to view and download files that are relevant to them.
* Sensitive data privacy / security requirements mean access to certain documents should be restricted for both front stage and back stage users.

## Considered Options

1. Use active directory groups to manage all access to Azure Blob Storage containers
2. Use active directory groups to manage access to Azure Blob Storage containers for back stage users, create Shared Access Signature tokens for front stage users to provide temporary access, and also leverage SAS tokens for special sensitive document access
human_decision:
# 7. Provide access to permit case file documents (Azure Blob storage) via Active Directory groups & Shared Access Signature tokens

* Status: proposed
* Deciders: 
* Date: 2019-10-03

## Decision Outcome

[Option 2] Using a combination of Active Directory groups and SAS tokens provides the greatest flexibility to manage document access. 

### Positive Consequences

* Front stage users are only able to access documents that are relevant to them, this would be managed by the frontend application & the document type attribute stored in the CRM / document metadata.
* Using SAS tokens for sensitive documents also simplifies the private access.

### Negative Consequences

* SAS tokens would potentially need be generated for each file download.

## Pros and Cons of the Options

### 1. Use active directory groups to manage all access to Azure Blob Storage containers

Active Directory groups can be assigned security roles on each container in Azure Blob Storage, therefore inheriting access from the Active Directory login.

#### Positive
* No need to grant any additional access permissions on files.
* Active Directory groups are already proposed for separating user and admin roles for both front and back stage.

#### Negative
* Multiple permit 'containers' would potentially be required to separate front stage files, back stage only files and any sensitive files. 

### 2. Use active directory groups to manage access to Azure Blob Storage containers for back stage users, create Shared Access Signature tokens for front stage users to provide temporary access, and also leverage SAS tokens for special sensitive document access

Access to all non sensitive documents for back stage users is managed via Active Directory groups & roles.  When front stage users try to download any files within the container, the application will generate a temporary SAS token that allows the user to access the file.  A similar process can also be used to grant temporary access to specific 'sensitive' files for back stage users when required.

#### Positive
* When combined with Dynamics / metadata file type look up by the application, this solution provides the most flexibility for file access.
* Maintains private managed access to all files.
* Temporary / managed access to sensitive data is more easily managed.

#### Negative
* Temporary access may require additional management from SEPA.

## Links

* [Authorize access to Azure blobs and queues using Azure Active Directory](https://docs.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/storage/common/storage-auth-aad)
* [Grant limited access to Azure Storage resources using shared access signatures (SAS)](https://docs.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/storage/common/storage-sas-overview)
debate_answer: Option 2 is the most suitable approach for managing access to permit application files in Azure Blob Storage.
reason: The affirmative side provides a comprehensive solution that effectively addresses the key requirements of authentication, authorization, data privacy, temporary access, and sensitive document access. It leverages the strengths of both active directory groups and SAS tokens to manage access effectively in Azure Blob Storage. While the negative side raises valid concerns about complexity, scalability, security risks, user experience, and cost implications, the affirmative side's solution is robust and well-suited to meet the system's requirements, considering security, compliance, and usability.
message_history:
The proposed solution, Option 2, appears to be the most suitable approach for managing access to permit application files in Azure Blob Storage based on the provided context. This solution effectively addresses the key requirements of authentication, authorization, data privacy, temporary access, and sensitive document access. Let's break down why Option 2 is the best choice:

1. **Authentication**: By using active directory groups, the system ensures that only authorized users can access the 'case file' documents. This is a robust method for managing user identities and access permissions.

2. **Authorization**: The implementation of role-based access control allows for differentiated access levels. Back stage users have unrestricted access, while front stage users have restricted access to only relevant files. This ensures that users only have access to the information necessary for their roles.

3. **Data Privacy**: The use of active directory groups and SAS tokens helps adhere to data privacy and security requirements. Access to sensitive documents is restricted based on user roles, ensuring compliance with privacy standards.

4. **Temporary Access**: SAS tokens provide a mechanism for granting temporary access to files for front stage users. This is particularly useful for scenarios where users need time-limited access to specific documents.

5. **Sensitive Document Access**: The additional layer of security for sensitive documents, through SAS tokens or other access control mechanisms, ensures that these documents are protected from unauthorized access.

Overall, Option 2 provides a comprehensive and balanced solution that meets the system's requirements while considering security, compliance, and usability. It leverages the strengths of both active directory groups and SAS tokens to manage access effectively in Azure Blob Storage.
While Option 2 presents a well-rounded approach, there are potential areas where it might not be the most optimal solution, and alternative strategies could be considered. Here are some reasons why one might disagree with the assertion that Option 2 is the best solution:

1. **Complexity and Management Overhead**: Implementing active directory groups and managing SAS tokens can introduce significant complexity and administrative overhead. Organizations with limited IT resources might find it challenging to maintain and update these systems, especially as user roles and access needs change over time.

2. **Scalability Concerns**: As the number of users and documents grows, the management of active directory groups and SAS tokens could become cumbersome. Alternative solutions that offer more automated or dynamic access control mechanisms might be more scalable in the long run.

3. **Security Risks with SAS Tokens**: While SAS tokens provide temporary access, they can also pose security risks if not managed properly. Tokens can be shared or leaked, potentially leading to unauthorized access. More secure alternatives, such as Azure Managed Identities or Azure AD Conditional Access, might offer better protection against such risks.

4. **User Experience**: The use of SAS tokens for front stage users might complicate the user experience, requiring additional steps to obtain and use these tokens. A more seamless integration with existing authentication systems could enhance usability.

5. **Cost Implications**: Depending on the organization's Azure setup, there might be cost implications associated with using active directory and SAS tokens. Exploring cost-effective alternatives that still meet security and compliance requirements could be beneficial.

In light of these considerations, it might be worthwhile to explore other options that offer simpler management, enhanced security, and better scalability, while still meeting the core requirements of authentication, authorization, data privacy, and temporary access.

## Context and Problem Statement

People have mentioned they want to be able to insert placeholders. The placeholders will then be also present in their editing tool of their choice, i.e. Sadie (ADL, EDL) or FCP. Since this is not a prioritised feature (it is a nice-to-have), it has been placed out-of-scope for now. Also "Placeholders" features are sometimes not used at all by content producers.

## Decision Drivers <!-- optional -->

* Ease of implementation
* User Experience
* Demand of feature

## Considered Options

1. Add Placeholder configuration to each type of export files
2. Add dummy content where the content will be replaced
3. Add dummy content with UI to specify filename, so when importing it will point to the right place
human_decision:
# Placeholder Media in Programme Script

* Status: accepted
* Deciders: Eimi, Pietro
* Date: 2019-10-01

Technical Story: [Investigate: Voiceover time buffer (EDL / FCPX)](https://github.com/bbc/digital-paper-edit-client/issues/65) and [UX investigations: how to better signpost Voice over/link](https://github.com/bbc/digital-paper-edit-client/issues/63)

## Decision Outcome

This implementation needs investigation from a technical and UX point of view.
We need to clear up assumptions about how things work technically by trying out the placeholder features of each editing tool.
We also need to clear up assumptions about how people use the placeholders, if it needs to be presented in a certain way in the Workspace view.
Ideally I would say Option 1, but we will need to investigate further to conclude properly. This document needs updating once investigations have been done.

### Positive Consequences <!-- optional -->

* Placeholder works out of the box for specific editing tools

### Negative consequences <!-- optional -->

* Will require reverse engineering to understand specific editing tools

## Pros and Cons of the Options <!-- optional -->

### Add "Placeholder" data for specific tools

This means creating solutions for specific editing tools - most likely three (EDL, ADL, FCPX).

* Good, because very specific, could potentially integrate existing flows
* Bad, because it's specific and that requires resources, such as access to the tools and OS to test solutions

### Add dummy data to be specified later

Add dummy data with default content, which will be added into the export file.
This will need to be replaced by the user manually and this could be a frustrating process.

* Good, because dummy data works with all editing tools.
* Bad, because you will have to replace the content later and that could mean you'd have to fiddle quite a bit in the editing tool (assumption)
* Bad, because have to create and store dummy data. (Unknown)

### Add dummy data and specify

Add dummy data with default content, which at export will be replaced with the name provided by the user.
This will require an interface where the user specifies at the point of creating the placeholder.

* Good, because dummy data works with all all editing tools.
* Good, because you can specify what the filename is.
* Bad, because specifying the filename not work out of the box. (Unknown)
* Bad, because you still might need the above dummy data.
debate_answer: Option 3: Add dummy content with UI to specify filename should be considered as the best proposed solution from the outset.
reason: The negative side presents a compelling argument for starting with Option 3 due to its focus on user experience, collaboration, and future-proofing. While it requires more initial effort, it offers a comprehensive solution that can lead to better long-term success and user satisfaction. The emphasis on providing a holistic solution and gaining meaningful feedback from users early on strengthens the case for Option 3 as the best proposed solution.
message_history:
In deciding the best proposed solution for implementing placeholders in export files, we need to consider the priorities of ease of use, compatibility, adaptability, performance, collaboration, and user experience. The recommended phased approach suggests starting with Option 1 and potentially moving to Options 2 and 3 based on feedback and demand. Let's evaluate each option:

1. **Option 1: Add Placeholder configuration to each type of export files** - This option is a good starting point as it addresses compatibility and adaptability. It allows for a straightforward implementation that can be easily adapted to different file types. This option is likely to be less complex and can be implemented quickly, making it a practical initial step.

2. **Option 2: Add dummy content where the content will be replaced** - This option introduces dummy content, which can be useful for visualizing where content will be placed. However, it may increase complexity and affect performance. It should be considered as an additional feature rather than a primary solution, especially if the impact on performance is significant.

3. **Option 3: Add dummy content with UI to specify filename** - This option offers a comprehensive solution that enhances user experience and collaboration by allowing users to specify filenames directly. However, it requires more development effort and could introduce additional complexity. It is best suited for scenarios where user experience and collaboration are top priorities and where the additional complexity can be managed effectively.

Given these considerations, the best approach is to start with **Option 1** to quickly address compatibility and adaptability needs. This allows for a foundation to be built upon, with the potential to incorporate **Option 2** and **Option 3** based on user feedback and specific project requirements. This phased approach ensures that the solution evolves in response to actual user needs while balancing ease of implementation and feature demand.
While the phased approach starting with Option 1 seems logical, I would argue that **Option 3: Add dummy content with UI to specify filename** should be considered as the best proposed solution from the outset, despite its complexity and development effort. Here are my reasons:

1. **User Experience and Collaboration**: Option 3 directly addresses user experience and collaboration, which are crucial for ensuring that the solution is not only functional but also user-friendly. By allowing users to specify filenames directly, it reduces the potential for errors and confusion, enhancing the overall user experience.

2. **Future-Proofing**: Implementing a comprehensive solution from the beginning can save time and resources in the long run. While it may require more initial effort, it avoids the need for significant changes or overhauls later on, which can be disruptive and costly.

3. **Holistic Solution**: Option 3 provides a more holistic solution by integrating UI elements that facilitate user interaction and collaboration. This can lead to better adoption and satisfaction among users, as they have more control and clarity over the export process.

4. **Feedback and Iteration**: Starting with a more comprehensive solution allows for more meaningful feedback from users, as they can experience the full range of features and provide insights on what works and what doesn't. This can lead to more targeted and effective iterations.

5. **Competitive Advantage**: Offering a robust and user-friendly solution from the start can provide a competitive advantage, as it demonstrates a commitment to quality and user satisfaction. This can be a key differentiator in a crowded market.

In conclusion, while Option 1 offers a quick and easy starting point, Option 3 provides a more comprehensive and future-proof solution that prioritizes user experience and collaboration. By investing in a more robust solution from the beginning, the project can achieve greater long-term success and user satisfaction.

## Context and Problem Statement

play-frontend-hmrc relies on a webjar for [hmrc/hmrc-frontend](https://www.github.com/hmrc/hmrc-frontend)
published to www.webjars.org. This has a number of drawbacks:
* publishing is a manual process
* it can take many hours to complete
* webjars has been known to be down and HMRC has no support arrangements with www.webjars.org

The main impact of the above is an excessive lead time for making improvements in the
underlying hmrc-frontend library available in production via play-frontend-hmrc.

Bearing the above in mind, and the fact that HMRC has its own repository for open artefacts, replacing
Bintray, should we:

* automate the creation of the webjars within our own deployment pipelines with no dependency
  on webjars.org
* publish the resulting webjars to this repository automatically?

Note, this decision only addresses the creation and publishing of the hmrc-frontend webjar, not the
webjar for [alphagov/govuk-frontend](https://www.github.com/alphagov/govuk-frontend), which is 
currently a dependency for [hmrc/play-frontend-govuk](https://www.github.com/hmrc/play-frontend-govuk).

## Decision Drivers

* The need to make improvements and upgrades to hmrc-frontend 
  available in play-frontend-hmrc quickly.
* The increasing user base of play-frontend-hmrc, and accelerating demand for new features and
improvements.
* The hardship, frustration and toil the current manual process is causing the team.
* The need to keep things simple and avoidance of creating new repositories unnecessarily due to
  the overhead of maintaining those repositories
* The testing advantages of being able to build and deploy the hmrc-frontend webjar locally
* Parity between the hmrc-frontend NPM package and the webjar.

## Considered Options

* Option 1: do nothing
* Option 2: auto-create and deploy webjars to HMRC's open artefact repository using a gulp task in 
  hmrc-frontend operating on the generated package in `/package`
* Option 3: as option 2 but operating on the downloaded package from NPM instead
* Option 4: as option 2 but via a separate Github repository that has `hmrc-frontend` as a
dependency and operating on `node_modules/hmrc-frontend` instead
human_decision: # Self-publish the hmrc-frontend webjar used by play-frontend-hmrc * Status: accepted * Date: 2020-06-07 Technical Story: PLATUI-1190 ## Decision Outcome Chosen option: option 2 because it solves the core issue and enables local testing without introducing additional dependencies. ### Existing architecture ### To be architecture ### Positive Consequences * Webjars are available instantaneously after a new version of hmrc-frontend is released * It is now possible to locally test changes to hmrc-frontend in conjunction with Scala microservices without needing to publish to NPM or webjars.org first, reducing the risk that flawed components are released into production. * Lead times for making improvements to hmrc-frontend available in production are reduced. * Maintaining play-frontend-hmrc is a less frustrating process. * We have more control over the metadata attached to the webjars published. For example, at the moment, the webjars produced indicate webjars.org as the developer in the POM files. * There are fewer external dependencies and moving parts. ### Negative Consequences * We have an additional moving part to maintain ourselves. ## Pros and Cons of the Options ### Option 1 * Good, because no further work required * Bad, because it doesn't solve the problem ### Option 2 * Good, because it's simple. * Good, because it doesn't rely on a new Github repository * Good, because it allows for local testing in Scala microservices * Good, because parity with published NPM package – the `/package` subdirectory is a carbon copy of the published NPM package ### Option 3 * Good, because it more faithfully replicates the previous webjars.org functionality * Bad, because it's more complex and requires more engineering and testing to extract from NPM e.g. timeouts/retries. * Bad, because it precludes the possibility of local testing of the webjar in Scala microservices. ### Option 4 * Bad, because an additional repository is needed that will need maintaining and security patching. * Bad, because additional wiring needed in Jenkins to kick off the webjar generation logic. * Bad, because it precludes the possibility of local testing of the webjar in Scala microservices. * Bad, because there is an additional step needed to bump the version of hmrc-frontend in the new repository. * Bad, because failures in webjar generation will not be picked up by PR status checks. * Bad, because lack of parity with published NPM package. The package.json in `node_modules/govuk-frontend` is different to the one published in NPM, it contains underscored properties that potentially contain sensitive CI-related information. However, we could strip these out if necessary. ## Links * https://www.webjars.org/documentation * https://www.playframework.com/documentation/2.8.x/AssetsOverview
debate_answer: Option 2: Auto-create and deploy webjars using a gulp task in hmrc-frontend is the recommended solution.
reason: The affirmative side presents a solution that is simpler and more reliable by leveraging existing infrastructure and minimizing maintenance overhead. Option 2 aligns well with current systems and processes, ensuring parity with NPM packages without introducing unnecessary complexity. While the negative side offers valid points about flexibility and direct integration with NPM, the potential complexity and initial setup challenges may outweigh the benefits in this context. Therefore, Option 2 is the more practical and efficient choice given the current requirements and constraints.
message_history:
In evaluating the proposed solutions for automating the creation and deployment of webjars, the recommendation is to adopt **Option 2: Auto-create and deploy webjars using a gulp task in hmrc-frontend**. This option is favored for several reasons:

1. **Automation and Efficiency**: Option 2 automates the process, significantly reducing the time and effort required compared to the current manual method. This automation helps in minimizing human error and streamlining the workflow.

2. **Simplicity and Reliability**: By using a gulp task within the existing hmrc-frontend infrastructure, this approach maintains simplicity and reliability. It avoids the need for additional tools or complex configurations, making it easier to implement and maintain.

3. **Leverage Existing Infrastructure**: This option takes advantage of HMRC's existing open artefact repository, which is already in place to replace Bintray. This ensures that the solution is well-integrated with current systems and processes.

4. **Parity with NPM Packages**: Ensuring parity with NPM packages is crucial for consistency and compatibility. Option 2 achieves this by aligning the webjars creation and deployment process with the standards and practices used for NPM packages.

5. **Minimal Maintenance Overhead**: By not introducing new repositories or dependencies, Option 2 minimizes the maintenance overhead. This is important for long-term sustainability and resource management.

While Option 2 appears to be the most suitable solution, it is important to conduct further evaluation and discussion to ensure that it fully addresses all requirements and constraints. This includes considering any potential challenges or limitations that may arise during implementation.
While Option 2 presents a compelling case for automating the creation and deployment of webjars, I would argue that **Option 3: Auto-create and deploy webjars by operating on the downloaded package from NPM** might be a more robust solution. Here are the reasons for considering Option 3:

1. **Direct Integration with NPM**: By operating directly on the downloaded package from NPM, Option 3 ensures that the webjars are always in sync with the latest versions available on NPM. This direct integration can reduce the risk of discrepancies between the NPM packages and the webjars.

2. **Flexibility and Future-Proofing**: Option 3 provides greater flexibility by decoupling the webjars creation process from the hmrc-frontend. This separation can make it easier to adapt to future changes in either the NPM packages or the hmrc-frontend without affecting the other.

3. **Potential for Broader Application**: By not tying the process to a specific frontend infrastructure, Option 3 could potentially be applied to other projects or teams within HMRC, making it a more versatile solution.

4. **Reduced Dependency on Specific Tools**: While using a gulp task in hmrc-frontend is straightforward, it does create a dependency on a specific tool and infrastructure. Option 3, by focusing on the NPM package, might allow for more flexibility in choosing tools and processes.

5. **Alignment with Industry Practices**: Many organizations are moving towards solutions that directly leverage package managers like NPM for their build and deployment processes. Option 3 aligns with this trend, potentially making it easier to integrate with other modern development practices.

While Option 3 may introduce some initial complexity in setting up the process to work directly with NPM packages, the long-term benefits of flexibility, direct integration, and potential for broader application could outweigh these challenges. As with any solution, thorough evaluation and testing would be necessary to ensure it meets all requirements and constraints effectively.

Therefore, Option 2 is the more practical and efficient choice given the current requirements and constraints.", "debate_answer": "Option 2: Auto-create and deploy webjars using a gulp task in hmrc-frontend is the recommended solution."}

## Context and Problem Statement

We want a message broker so the devices and other applications can communicate with the backend.

What broker and protocol should we use?

## Decision Drivers <!-- optional -->

- Applicability regarding IoT projects : low-resources clients, etc
- Possibility to use it to stream frames/images coming from cars cameras
- Ease of deployment on Kubernetes
- Existing knowledge of the team

## Considered Options

- [RabbitMQ](https://www.rabbitmq.com/), optionally with [MQTT plugin](https://www.rabbitmq.com/mqtt.html)
- [VerneMQ](https://vernemq.com/)
- Non-MQTT brokers
    - [Kafka](https://kafka.apache.org/)
    - [NATS](https://nats.io/)
    - [AWS Kinesis](https://aws.amazon.com/kinesis/)
    - [Google Cloud Pub/Sub](https://cloud.google.com/pubsub/)
human_decision:
# Use RabbitMQ with MQTT plugin as message broker for devices so they can communicate with each other

* Status: Accepted
- Date: 2019-03-11
- Deciders:
    - achotard
    - blacroix
    - jmartinsanchez

## Decision Outcome

Chosen option: **[RabbitMQ](TODO) with [MQTT plugin](https://www.rabbitmq.com/mqtt.html)**, because:

- It is already well-known among the team
- It has some [existing "official" Helm chart](https://github.com/helm/charts/tree/master/stable/rabbitmq)
- It seems like a good fit to iterate fast

We **do not exclude switching to another MQTT broker such as VerneMQ in the future**, depending on our ability to dsitribute it cleanly on Kubernetes.

We also **do not exclude using another broker such as Kafka or NATS for appropriate use cases**.

## Pros and Cons of the Options <!-- optional -->

### RabbitMQ

Pros:

- Existing ["official" Helm chart](https://github.com/helm/charts/tree/master/stable/rabbitmq) ready to be deployed on Kubernetes
- Another ["official" Helm chart supporting High
  Availability](https://github.com/helm/charts/tree/master/stable/rabbitmq-ha/)
  RabbitMQ clusters on Kubernetes
- Official [MQTT plugin](https://www.rabbitmq.com/mqtt.html) supported in core distribution

Cons:

- Too obvious :D
- Doesn't scale as well as alternatives

### VerneMQ

Pros:

- Is the most performant MQTT broker out there
- Is known to be scalable
- Is pretty cool

Cons:

- No "official" Helm chart ready to be deployed on Kubernetes
- Not existing knowledge about it

### Non-MQTT brokers

Non-MQTT message brokers such as NATS, Kafka, AWS Kinesis and Google Cloud Pub/Sub were quickly put aside due to their lack of support for the MQTT protocol.

**Managed services** such as Kinesis or Pub/Sub are also eliminated because they would lock us with a given provider and we want to be able to host the entire XebiKart infrastructure anywhere (envisionning going multi-cloud). This is also the reason behind the lack of consideration for things such as **AWS IoT** or equivalent MQTT managed solutions.

**Kafka** didn't look like a good candidate for our use-case (also not supporting MQTT), as well as native NATS even if this one is the most ready to be distributed on Kubernetes. **We are still considering both for a later stage** when we will be deeper in the project and when we will be able to split brokers according to their usage.
debate_answer: The best proposed solution is to prioritize cloud-native integration, scalability, and ease of management, focusing on AWS Kinesis or Google Cloud Pub/Sub for cloud-native applications, Kafka for scalability and performance, and NATS for IoT and real-time communication, without extensive proof-of-concept testing.
reason: The negative side provides a more streamlined approach by focusing on overarching considerations such as cloud-native integration, scalability, and ease of management. This approach simplifies the decision-making process without the need for extensive proof-of-concept testing, which can be resource-intensive. The negative side effectively highlights the importance of aligning with broader project goals and constraints, making it a more practical solution for teams with limited resources.
message_history:
The decision on the best message broker solution from the provided analysis depends heavily on the specific requirements and constraints of the project in question. Here are some considerations to help decide:

1. **IoT Device Communication**: If the primary focus is on IoT device communication, RabbitMQ with MQTT Plugin, VerneMQ, or NATS are strong candidates. RabbitMQ with MQTT Plugin and VerneMQ are particularly suited for handling streaming data and large payloads over MQTT, while NATS offers lightweight protocol support for low-resource devices and real-time communication.

2. **High-Bandwidth Streaming Data**: For projects that require handling high-bandwidth streaming data, Kafka stands out due to its excellent scalability and performance, making it ideal for large-scale distributed systems. NATS, AWS Kinesis, and Google Cloud Pub/Sub are also viable options, with the latter two being particularly suited for cloud-native applications.

3. **Cloud-Native Applications**: If the project is heavily invested in a cloud ecosystem, AWS Kinesis and Google Cloud Pub/Sub are ideal choices. They offer managed services that simplify deployment, although additional setup may be needed for IoT devices.

4. **Real-Time Processing**: NATS is specifically highlighted for real-time processing needs, making it a strong contender if this is a critical requirement.

5. **Large-Scale Distributed Systems**: Kafka is the recommended choice for large-scale distributed systems due to its scalability and performance capabilities.

Ultimately, the best solution will align with the project's specific needs, such as the type of data being communicated, the need for real-time processing, existing team expertise, and any investment in a particular cloud ecosystem. Conducting a thorough evaluation or proof-of-concept with each option is advisable to determine the most suitable message broker for the use case.
While I agree with the general approach of aligning the choice of message broker with specific project needs, I would argue that there are some overarching considerations that might simplify the decision-making process without needing to conduct a proof-of-concept for each option.

1. **Cloud-Native Preference**: If the project is already heavily invested in a cloud ecosystem, the choice should lean towards AWS Kinesis or Google Cloud Pub/Sub. These services not only offer seamless integration with other cloud services but also provide managed solutions that can significantly reduce operational overhead. This can be a decisive factor for teams looking to minimize infrastructure management and focus on application development.

2. **Scalability and Performance**: For projects where scalability and performance are paramount, Kafka should be the default choice. Its proven track record in handling high-throughput and large-scale distributed systems makes it a reliable option. While NATS and other brokers can handle high-bandwidth data, Kafka's ecosystem and community support provide additional tools and frameworks that can enhance data processing capabilities.

3. **IoT and Real-Time Communication**: For IoT applications, while RabbitMQ with MQTT Plugin and VerneMQ are strong contenders, NATS offers a unique advantage with its lightweight protocol and real-time communication capabilities. This makes it particularly suitable for environments with low-resource devices and the need for immediate data processing.

4. **Ease of Deployment and Management**: If ease of deployment and management is a critical factor, especially in Kubernetes environments, AWS Kinesis and Google Cloud Pub/Sub again stand out due to their managed nature. This can be a significant advantage for teams with limited DevOps resources.

In conclusion, while a detailed evaluation is beneficial, focusing on the broader project goals and constraints can help streamline the decision-making process. By prioritizing cloud-native integration, scalability, and ease of management, teams can make a more informed choice without the need for extensive proof-of-concept testing across all options.

## Context and Problem Statement

A solution for isolating third-party payment widgets from the rest of account
management can include building a separate web app on its own dedicated domain.
As a side effect in building that app, we have the opportunity to choose
technologies for building it that don't necessarily follow the rest of FxA.

## Decision Drivers

* Opportunity for a fresh start with tech stack without rewriting
* Security in dealing with payment transactions
* Developer ergonomics
* Code quality & testing
* Subscription services deadlines

## Considered Options

* React, Redux, Typescript
* Backbone.js
human_decision:
# Use React, Redux, and Typescript for subscription management pages

* Deciders: Ben Bangert, Ian Bicking, Les Orchard
* Date: 2019-05-08

## Decision Outcome

Chosen option: "React, Redux, Typescript", because

* Chance to start with a fresh stack
* More vibrant ecosystem
* Better tooling & developer ergonomics

## Pros and Cons of the Options

### React, Redux, TypeScript

[React][] is a library for building DOM-based UI components in a data-driven,
declarative style that minimizes granular DOM manipulation by developers by way
of a Virtual DOM diff / patch algorithm. It also introduces patterns & tools for
encapsulating reusable UI functionality in "hooks" and JS classes.

[React]: https://reactjs.org/

[Redux][] is a library for managing app state in a singleton container using a
defined inventory of actions that describe changes to be applied via reducer
functions and middleware filters.

[Redux]: https://redux.js.org/

[TypeScript][] is a superset of JavaScript which introduces type annotation
expressions and type inference along with tooling to lint and compile.

[TypeScript]: https://www.typescriptlang.org/

We can also consider other dependencies that act as glue between these pieces
and utilities to reduce boilerplate and repetition. (e.g. [redux-actions][] to
simplify constructing actions; [redux-promise-middleware][] for actions that
result in async API fetches; [redux-thunk][] for producing action sequences)

[redux-actions]: https://redux-actions.js.org/ 

[redux-promise-middleware]:
https://github.com/pburtchaell/redux-promise-middleware 

[redux-thunk]: https://github.com/reduxjs/redux-thunk

* Pros
  * Frequently updated and vibrant ecosystem
  * New team members may be more familiar with the React / Redux / TypeScript
    ecosystem
  * React integrates well with Webpack for code-splitting and lazy-loaded
    component JS bundles
  * React and Webpack can support per-component hot reloading rather than
    reloading the whole page
  * React can organize UI rendering into small components with single
    responsibility for each
  * React hooks can be used to encapsulate reusable chunks of UI logic & state
    between components
  * React offers [safeguards][react-innerhtml] against accidental XSS from
    unescaped HTML.
  * Redux can organize business logic and API requests in a central set of
    modules
  * Data-driven React components can be loosely-coupled with Redux state
  * The Redux dev tools browser add-on can monitor, inspect, and even rewind app
    state changes
  * TypeScript can help document & validate data structures at build time (e.g.
    in Redux state) and integrate with IDEs (e.g. intellisense hints in VS Code)
  * Can follow the Mocha / Chai / Sinon style unit testing stack used elsewhere
    in the project
  * Can use [React bindings][fluent-react] for [fluent.js][fluent] and [FTL][]
    strings for L10n.
* Cons
  * Novel technology stack for the project in general.
  * Fast-moving tech stack that may need chasing after.
  * Reduced code sharing with the rest of the project, reinventing some wheels.
  * Fluent is a research project by Mozilla, [it has occasionally presented
    challenges][fluent-challenges].
  
[fluent]: https://projectfluent.org/
[fluent-react]: https://github.com/projectfluent/fluent.js/wiki/React-Bindings
[ftl]: https://projectfluent.org/fluent/guide/
[fluent-challenges]: https://github.com/mozilla/testpilot/issues?utf8=%E2%9C%93&q=is%3Aissue+is%3Aclosed+fluent
[react-innerhtml]: https://reactjs.org/docs/dom-elements.html#dangerouslysetinnerhtml

### Backbone.js

[Backbone.js][] is small framework that offers base View and Model abstractions.

[Backbone.js]: https://backbonejs.org/

FxA combines it with a few other utilities including [Mustache][] for HTML
templates; [Cocktail][] for class mixins; [jQuery][] for DOM manipulation.
Depends on [Underscore.js][] for general data manipulation.

[Mustache]: https://mustache.github.io/ 

[Cocktail]: https://github.com/onsi/cocktail

[jQuery]: https://jquery.com/

[Underscore.js]: https://underscorejs.org/

* Pros
  * Framework of record for client-side code in FxA so far.
  * Granular DOM updates in View classes do not rely on an abstract Virtual DOM
    that can have an impact on performance.
  * Pretty small framework, even with the added utilities.
  * Mature framework that doesn't need much chasing after.
  * Lots of mature tested code within FxA to reuse and on which to model new
    additions.
  * Uses [mature gettext tools & processes for L10n][fxa-l10n].
  * Mustache and Backbone best practices help guard against XSS via unescaped HTML.
* Cons
  * New team members may not be familiar with Backbone patterns
  * Updating Views requires granular DOM changes that can sometime collide
    between different Views that affect the same parts of the DOM - i.e
    overlapping responsibilities.
  * Business logic can be scattered amongst separate Model classes.
  * Infrequent releases: v1.4.0 released on 2019-02, but v1.3.3 released on
    2016-04.
  * fxa-content-server uses v1.1.1 from 2014-02 along with similarly-aged
    versions of Cocktail and Mustache.
  * Fluent [was created][fluent-created] to address issues [found over years of using
    gettext][fluent-v-gettext] at Mozilla.

[fxa-l10n]: https://github.com/mozilla/fxa-content-server-l10n/
[fluent-created]: https://hacks.mozilla.org/2019/04/fluent-1-0-a-localization-system-for-natural-sounding-translations/
[fluent-v-gettext]: https://github.com/projectfluent/fluent/wiki/Fluent-vs-gettext
debate_answer: Conduct a detailed assessment of the project's requirements, team skills, and long-term objectives to select the most appropriate technology stack.
reason: The decision between React, Redux, TypeScript and Backbone.js should be based on a comprehensive evaluation of the project's specific needs, team expertise, and long-term goals. React, Redux, and TypeScript offer strong security features, scalability, and community support, making them suitable for complex projects with experienced teams. Backbone.js, being lightweight and easier to learn, is more suitable for simpler projects or teams with limited resources. The best solution depends on aligning the technology stack with the project's requirements and the team's capabilities.
message_history:
In deciding the best proposed solution between React, Redux, TypeScript (Option 1) and Backbone.js (Option 2), we need to consider several factors such as the team's expertise, project requirements, and long-term maintenance considerations.

**Option 1: React, Redux, TypeScript**

Pros:
1. **Strong security features**: This is crucial for applications handling sensitive payment information.
2. **Excellent community support**: A large and active community can be invaluable for troubleshooting and finding solutions to common problems.
3. **Robust ecosystem for developer tools**: This makes development, testing, and debugging more efficient.
4. **Scalable architecture**: Important for applications that need to handle increased traffic during peak periods.

Cons:
1. **Steeper learning curve**: This could slow down development if the team is not familiar with these technologies.

**Option 2: Backbone.js**

Pros:
1. **Lightweight**: Easier to develop and maintain applications.
2. **Flexible**: Allows for building applications that meet specific requirements.
3. **Easier to learn**: Beneficial for teams without prior experience in React.

Cons:
1. **Less built-in support for modern web development best practices**: This could be a limitation for developing modern applications.
2. **Smaller community**: Makes it harder to find solutions to common problems.

**Decision:**

If the team has experience with React or TypeScript, Option 1 is likely the better choice due to its robust security features, scalable architecture, and strong community support. These factors are particularly important for applications that handle sensitive data and require scalability.

However, if the team is new to both options and needs to get up to speed quickly, Backbone.js might be a more practical choice due to its ease of learning and flexibility, despite its limitations in terms of community support and modern best practices.

Ultimately, the decision should be based on the team's current expertise and the specific needs of the project. If security and scalability are top priorities and the team can manage the learning curve, React, Redux, and TypeScript would be the recommended choice. If ease of learning and quick adaptation are more critical, Backbone.js could be considered.
While I understand the reasoning behind choosing React, Redux, and TypeScript (Option 1) for their robust security features, scalability, and strong community support, I would argue that Backbone.js (Option 2) might be a more suitable choice in certain scenarios, even if the team has some experience with React or TypeScript. Here are my reasons:

1. **Project Complexity and Requirements**: If the project is relatively simple and does not require the full power of React's ecosystem, Backbone.js could be a more efficient choice. Its lightweight nature can lead to faster development and easier maintenance, which is beneficial for smaller projects or those with limited resources.

2. **Development Speed**: In situations where time-to-market is critical, the ease of learning and flexibility offered by Backbone.js can be advantageous. Teams can quickly adapt and start building without the overhead of mastering a more complex stack like React, Redux, and TypeScript.

3. **Resource Constraints**: If the team is small or has limited resources, the simplicity and lower overhead of Backbone.js can be a significant advantage. It allows developers to focus on delivering features rather than managing the complexities of a larger framework.

4. **Long-term Maintenance**: While React has a strong community, it also evolves rapidly, which can lead to frequent updates and changes. For teams that prefer stability and minimal disruption, Backbone.js's slower pace of change might be more appealing.

5. **Specific Use Cases**: There are scenarios where the flexibility of Backbone.js is particularly beneficial, such as when integrating with existing systems or when specific architectural patterns are required that Backbone.js supports well.

In conclusion, while React, Redux, and TypeScript offer many advantages, Backbone.js should not be dismissed outright. It can be the better choice for projects with simpler requirements, tighter deadlines, or resource constraints. The decision should ultimately be based on a thorough assessment of the project's specific needs and the team's capacity to handle the chosen technology stack.

## Context and Problem Statement

In the context of classic services' requirement for contact HMRC and Welsh information links in their footer, facing the
 fact that these links are missing from hmrcStandardFooter, should we add them?

The additional links needed are:

* "Contact", linking to https://www.gov.uk/government/organisations/hm-revenue-customs/contact
* "Rhestr o Wasanaethau Cymraeg", linking to https://www.gov.uk/cymraeg

## Decision Drivers

* The need for consistency across HMRC services.
* Our belief that including them is likely to improve the user experience for tax users.
* We can see no good reason for not including them as standard because they are applicable across HMRC services.
* We have a time sensitive opportunity of acting now while teams are in the process of
uplifting their frontend libraries.
* The HMRC design community have been consulted on multiple public Slack channels and two
successive design system working group meetings, with no objections noted.
* Classic services support multiple live services. Not including these links as standard would mean their having to
duplicate these links, and associated English and Welsh content, across tens of repositories.

## Considered Options

* Add the links to hmrcStandardFooter
* Create an hmrcExtendedFooter component containing the additional links
* Do nothing
human_decision:
# Add contact HMRC and Welsh information links to standard footer

* Status: accepted
* Date: 2020-12-04

Technical Story: PLATUI-854

## Decision Outcome

Chosen option: "Add the links to hmrcStandardFooter", because this 
will benefit tax users, and we have a unique window of opportunity to act now.

### Positive Consequences

* Tax users have better information provided to them
* Teams do not need to duplicate content and URLs across hundreds of repositories
* We can more easily maintain the content and links in a central repository

### Negative Consequences

* Teams currently using a Welsh link as their language toggle will likely need to switch to using one of the standard components
for language switching e.g. hmrcLanguageSelect.
* Teams already including a contact link manually will need to remove it when upgrading. 

## Pros and Cons of the Options

### Add the links to hmrcStandardFooter

* Good, because it's more likely the additional links will become standard benefiting
users
* Good, because we can standardise the content for English and Welsh
* Good, because hyperlinks can be maintained in a central place and do not have to be corrected in 100s of separate services
* Bad, because it's possible some teams may not want a contact or Welsh link for valid business reasons

### Create an hmrcExtendedFooter component containing the additional links

* Good, because new links will be added consistently for teams adopting it
* Good, because we can standardise the text for English and Welsh
* Good, because the hyperlinks can be maintained in a central repository
* Bad, because uptake unlikely to be as high as making the links standard
* Bad, because teams may become confused about which footer to use, increasing delivery friction

### Do nothing

* Good, because teams have more flexibility
* Bad, because tax users will not benefit from these additional information links
* Bad, because teams may add the additional links inconsistently with different text, different URLs, or 
 in a different order
* Bad, because there is no standardisation of the content in English and Welsh
* Bad, because if changes need to be made, they will need to be made in 100s of individual services rather
than in one library
* Bad, because teams will have to rollback these changes if they adopt a new standard footer with the contact and Welsh links
debate_answer: Option 1: Add links to `hmrcStandardFooter` is the best proposed solution.
